macro_segments.title: Introduction to Parental Obligations (Mitzvot haben al ha'av) and the Mishnah's Framework

micro_segments.title: Overview of Daf 29: Transition from Kinyanim to Mitzvot Categories
The Mishnah's Four Categories: Parental obligations, children's obligations, time-bound positive commandments, and negative commandments
Gemara's Interpretation: 'Kol mitzvot haben al ha'av' refers to obligations fathers have toward their sons
Children's Obligations Toward Parents (Kibbud av va'em): Boys and girls, men and women are equally obligated

macro_segments.title: The Braita on Paternal Obligations: Brit Milah, Pidyon HaBen, Teaching Torah, and Additional Duties

micro_segments.title: The Five Specific Obligations: Circumcision, redemption of firstborn son, teaching Torah, arranging marriage, and teaching a trade
Rabbi Yehuda's Teaching on Trade: Failure to teach a trade is as if teaching banditry

macro_segments.title: Brit Milah: Source, Hierarchy of Obligation, and Exclusion of Women

micro_segments.title: Source and Primary Obligation: Father's duty derived from Abraham circumcising Isaac
Secondary Obligations: If father fails, community (beis din) is obligated; if community fails, adult male must self-circumcise
Exclusion of Women: Textual basis 'kaasher tziva oso' applies only to the father, not the mother
Proof from the School of Rabbi Ishmael: 'Kol makom shenemar tzav' implies commandment applies to all generations only as stated

macro_segments.title: Pidyon HaBen (Redemption of Firstborn Son): Textual Sources, Gender Distinctions, and Competing Obligations

micro_segments.title: Source and Hierarchy: Father redeems son; if father fails, son must redeem himself as adult
Exclusion of Women via Textual Parallel: 'Tifdeh tipadeh' implies those who redeem must be redeemable; women are not subjects of redemption ('banecha v'lo bnosecha')
Logical Chain: Only those obligated to be redeemed are obligated to redeem; only those obligated for themselves are obligated for others
Proof of Daughters' Exclusion: 'Kol bechor banecha tifdeh'—'banecha v'lo bnosecha' (sons and not daughters)

macro_segments.title: Competing Parental and Personal Obligations: Self vs. Son in Pidyon HaBen

micro_segments.title: First Opinion: Self comes first—the father's personal obligation to redeem himself takes precedence
Rabbi Yehuda's Counter-Position: Son comes first—the father's primary obligation is toward the son, since the father's obligation originated from his own father
Gemara's Reconciliation (Rav Yirmiyahu): Both agree that self comes first when only five sela'im exist (sufficient only for one)
The Ten Sela'im Dispute: Rival interpretations of liens (Rabbi Yehuda: Torah obligations create liens like a shtar; Rabbanan: Torah obligations do not)

macro_segments.title: Pidyon HaBen vs. Aliyah LaRegel: Prioritizing Non-Time-Bound Over Time-Bound Obligations

micro_segments.title: Consensus Opinion: Redeem son first, then make pilgrimage festival—non-repeatable obligation takes precedence
Rabbi Yehuda's Rationale: Go on pilgrimage first because it is time-bound (mitzvah overes); redemption can be done later
Rabbanan's Rationale: The sequential order of verses (redemption then pilgrimage) dictates priority

macro_segments.title: Multiple Firstborn Sons: Proof that Father Must Redeem All from Different Mothers

micro_segments.title: Source: 'Kol bechor banecha tifdeh' (all your firstborn sons) indicates plural firstborns must all be redeemed
Clarification: Firstborn status determined by mother, not father—each son is 'peter rechem' (opener of womb) to his mother

macro_segments.title: Teaching Torah (Talmud Torah): Paternal Obligation, Gender Exemption, and Self-Teaching

micro_segments.title: Source and Hierarchy: Father teaches son Torah; if father fails, son must teach himself
Exclusion of Women: 'V'limadetem osam es beneichem' (teach your sons)—'banecha v'lo bnosecha' (sons and not daughters)
Bidirectional Logic: Those obligated to learn are obligated to teach; those exempt from learning are exempt from teaching
Women's Exemption from Self-Teaching: Women not obligated to be taught by others, therefore not obligated to teach themselves or others
Modern Scholarly Discourse: Extensive debates since the 1950s (Rav Soloveitchik, Lubavitcher Rebbe, others) on women's exemption from Talmud Torah and implications for contemporary Jewish education
Midrashic Context: Emphasis on women's presence at Maamad Har Sinai and the principle of universal Torah transmission, complicating the technical exemption

macro_segments.title: Teaching Torah: Self vs. Son Priority and the Example of Rav Yaakov

micro_segments.title: Default Position: Self comes first—father's obligation to himself takes precedence in Torah study
Rabbi Yehuda's Refinement: If son is sharp and retains knowledge better, son's learning takes precedence (conditional on son's aptitude)
Exemplum: Rav Yaakov ben Rav Acha—Father sends son to Abaye, discovers son's learning is deficient, reverses the arrangement and goes to learn himself
Excursus: Abaye's Demon (Mazik) and Rav Acha's Miracle—Narrative about a demonic force in the yeshiva subdued when Rav Acha sleeps in the study hall

macro_segments.title: Summary: Paternal Obligations as Gender-Differentiated Duties and Balancing Principles

micro_segments.title: Scope of Paternal Duties: Brit Milah, Pidyon HaBen, Torah education, marriage arrangement, and trade training—all father-to-son obligations excluding mothers and daughters
Gender Exclusion Patterns: Consistent textual grounds for excluding women/daughters, with some minority opinions (Rashi, Mareh Or Zarua) suggesting maternal backup or obligation
Balancing Principles: Tension between personal obligation and filial obligation; competing duties resolved variously by priority, aptitude, and temporal constraints

topical_tags.term: Banecha v'lo bnosecha (sons and not daughters)
Kibbud av va'em (honor father and mother)
School of Rabbi Ishmael (Tana d'vei Rabi Yishmael)
Rav Soloveitchik and modern discourse on women's Torah study
Maamad Har Sinai (Revelation at Mount Sinai)
Rav Yaakov ben Rav Acha (exemplum of parental obligation)
